speaker: God
quote_text: Have you considered my servant Job?
prompt_context: In the heavenly council, the Lord drew attention to Job's character before the accuser spoke against him.
question: Who asked whether Satan had considered Job?

speaker: Satan
quote_text: Does Job fear God for nothing?
prompt_context: After God commended Job, the accuser challenged the sincerity of Job's devotion.
question: Who asked whether Job feared God for nothing?

speaker: God
quote_text: Behold, all that he has is in your hand
prompt_context: In answer to Satan's challenge, the Lord allowed Job's possessions to be tested while setting a limit.
question: Who told Satan that everything Job had was in his hand, but Job himself must not be touched?

speaker: Job
quote_text: The Lord gave
prompt_context: After losing his possessions and children, Job responded in worship rather than cursing God.
question: Who said that the Lord gave and the Lord had taken away?

speaker: God
quote_text: He still holds fast his integrity
prompt_context: After the first round of suffering, the Lord again pointed to Job's faithfulness before Satan.
question: Who said that Job still held fast his integrity although he had been incited against him?

speaker: Satan
quote_text: Skin for skin
prompt_context: In the second heavenly exchange, the accuser argued that deeper personal suffering would break Job's faith.
question: Who said, "Skin for skin," and urged that Job's flesh and bones be struck?

speaker: Job's Wife
quote_text: Curse God and die
prompt_context: After Job was struck with painful sores, even his wife despaired at his continued faithfulness.
question: Who told Job to curse God and die?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Shall we receive good from God?
prompt_context: In reply to his wife's despairing counsel, Job affirmed that people must also accept adversity from God's hand.
question: Who answered that people should receive both good and trouble from God?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Let the day perish
prompt_context: After seven days of silence, Job finally opened his mouth and cursed the day of his birth.
question: Who said, "Let the day perish," beginning the great lament?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Why did I not die at birth?
prompt_context: In his opening lament, Job questioned why his life had not ended before his suffering began.
question: Who asked why he did not die at birth?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: Who that was innocent ever perished?
prompt_context: The first friend to speak began by implying that suffering of this kind does not come upon the truly innocent.
question: Who asked Job who that was innocent had ever perished?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: Can mortal man be in the right before God?
prompt_context: Recalling a fearful vision in the night, Eliphaz stressed human impurity before the divine.
question: Who asked whether a mortal could be more righteous than God?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: As for me, I would seek God
prompt_context: After describing the fate of the foolish, Eliphaz urged a path of submission and appeal to God.
question: Who told Job that he, for his part, would seek God and commit his cause to him?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: Blessed is the one whom God reproves
prompt_context: Eliphaz interpreted Job's suffering as divine correction and urged him not to despise it.
question: Who said that the one whom God reproves is blessed?

speaker: Job
quote_text: My words have been rash
prompt_context: Answering Eliphaz, Job explained that the weight of his grief made his speech wild and bitter.
question: Who admitted that his words had been rash because his suffering was so heavy?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Teach me
prompt_context: Job challenged his friends to show him specifically where he was wrong instead of assuming hidden guilt.
question: Who said, "Teach me," and asked to be shown how he had gone astray?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Why have you made me your mark?
prompt_context: Turning directly toward God, Job wondered why he seemed singled out for relentless suffering.
question: Who asked God why he had made him his target?

speaker: Bildad
quote_text: Does God pervert justice?
prompt_context: Bildad responded by defending God's justice and implying that Job's household had suffered because of sin.
question: Who asked whether God perverts justice?

speaker: Bildad
quote_text: Inquire of bygone ages
prompt_context: Bildad appealed to ancient wisdom and the testimony of former generations to support his argument.
question: Who urged Job to inquire of former generations and consider what their fathers had found out?

speaker: Bildad
quote_text: God will not reject a blameless man
prompt_context: Bildad concluded his first speech by insisting that God does not cast off the upright.
question: Who said that God would not reject a blameless man nor take the hand of evildoers?

speaker: Job
quote_text: How can a man be in the right before God?
prompt_context: Replying to Bildad, Job agreed about God's greatness but wrestled with the impossibility of contending with him.
question: Who asked how a person could be right before God?

speaker: Job
quote_text: There is no arbiter between us
prompt_context: Job longed for someone who could mediate between himself and God and lay a hand on both sides.
question: Who lamented that there was no arbiter between him and God?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Does it seem good to you to oppress?
prompt_context: Still speaking to God, Job asked why the Creator seemed to destroy the very work of his own hands.
question: Who asked God whether it seemed good to oppress and despise the work of his hands?

speaker: Zophar
quote_text: Should a multitude of words go unanswered?
prompt_context: The third friend entered the debate by rebuking Job's many words and self-defense.
question: Who asked whether a multitude of words should go unanswered?

speaker: Zophar
quote_text: Can you find out the deep things of God?
prompt_context: Zophar emphasized God's unsearchable greatness and suggested that Job could not grasp divine wisdom.
question: Who asked whether Job could find out the deep things of God?

speaker: Zophar
quote_text: If you prepare your heart
prompt_context: Zophar offered Job a conditional promise of restoration if he would put away wrongdoing.
question: Who told Job that if he prepared his heart and put iniquity far away, he could lift up his face without blemish?

speaker: Job
quote_text: No doubt you are the people
prompt_context: Job answered Zophar with biting sarcasm about his friends' supposed monopoly on wisdom.
question: Who sarcastically said, "No doubt you are the people, and wisdom will die with you"?

speaker: Job
quote_text: I would speak to the Almighty
prompt_context: Job insisted that he wanted to take his case directly to God rather than rely on his friends' counsel.
question: Who said that he desired to reason with God himself?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Though he slay me
prompt_context: In the middle of deep anguish, Job still expressed determined trust while also insisting on arguing his ways before God.
question: Who said, "Though he slay me," while still hoping in God?

speaker: Job
quote_text: If a man dies, shall he live again?
prompt_context: Reflecting on mortality, Job wondered whether there could be hope beyond death.
question: Who asked, "If a man dies, shall he live again?"

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: Should a wise man answer with windy knowledge?
prompt_context: In his second speech, Eliphaz rebuked Job for words he thought were empty, dangerous, and irreverent.
question: Who asked whether a wise man should answer with windy knowledge?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: What is man, that he can be pure?
prompt_context: Continuing his argument, Eliphaz stressed the uncleanness of humanity in comparison with God.
question: Who asked what man was that he could be pure or righteous?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Miserable comforters are you all
prompt_context: Wearied by repeated accusations, Job accused his friends of failing him instead of helping him.
question: Who told his friends that they were all miserable comforters?

speaker: Job
quote_text: My witness is in heaven
prompt_context: Even while feeling abandoned on earth, Job insisted that there was a witness and advocate for him before God.
question: Who said that his witness was in heaven and he who testified for him was on high?

speaker: Bildad
quote_text: The light of the wicked indeed goes out
prompt_context: In his second speech, Bildad described in dark imagery the downfall that overtakes the wicked.
question: Who said that the light of the wicked goes out?

speaker: Job
quote_text: I know that my Redeemer lives
prompt_context: In one of his strongest statements of hope, Job looked beyond present suffering to vindication.
question: Who declared, "I know that my Redeemer lives"?

speaker: Zophar
quote_text: The exulting of the wicked is short
prompt_context: In his second speech, Zophar insisted that the triumph of the wicked is brief and ends in misery.
question: Who said that the joy of the godless is but for a moment?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Why do the wicked live?
prompt_context: Answering his friends' tidy explanations, Job pointed to the apparent prosperity of many evil people.
question: Who asked why the wicked live on and grow mighty in power?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: Is not your evil abundant?
prompt_context: In his final speech, Eliphaz moved from insinuation to direct accusations against Job's character.
question: Who bluntly asked Job whether his evil was not abundant?

speaker: Eliphaz
quote_text: Agree with God
prompt_context: After accusing Job, Eliphaz urged him to return to God and promised restoration.
question: Who urged Job to agree with God and be at peace?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Oh, that I knew where I might find him
prompt_context: Job longed to come before God directly and present his case, confident that he would be heard.
question: Who wished he knew where he might find God so he could come to his seat?

speaker: Job
quote_text: He knows the way that I take
prompt_context: Even in confusion, Job still believed that God knew him fully and that testing would finally reveal his integrity.
question: Who said that after being tested he would come forth as gold?

speaker: Bildad
quote_text: How then can man be in the right before God?
prompt_context: In his final brief speech, Bildad stressed again the impurity of man before the majesty of God.
question: Who asked how man could be in the right before God?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Till I die I will not put away my integrity
prompt_context: Job refused to accept his friends' verdict and held firmly to his innocence.
question: Who insisted that he would not put away his integrity until he died?

speaker: Job
quote_text: The fear of the Lord, that is wisdom
prompt_context: In the hymn to wisdom, the conclusion pointed away from human discovery and toward reverence before God.
question: Who said that the fear of the Lord is wisdom and turning from evil is understanding?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Oh, that I had one to hear me
prompt_context: At the climax of his self-defense, Job longed for a formal hearing and an answer from the Almighty.
question: Who cried out for someone to hear him and for the Almighty to answer him?

speaker: Elihu
quote_text: I am young in years
prompt_context: After Job's three friends stopped answering, a younger observer finally began to speak with deference and pent-up frustration.
question: Who said, "I am young in years," before entering the debate?

speaker: Elihu
quote_text: I am full of words
prompt_context: Explaining why he could no longer remain silent, Elihu compared himself to wine without vent.
question: Who said that he was full of words and had to speak to find relief?

speaker: Elihu
quote_text: God is greater than man
prompt_context: Elihu rebuked Job for contending with God and insisted that God owes explanations to no one.
question: Who told Job that God is greater than man?

speaker: Elihu
quote_text: Far be it from God that he should do wickedness
prompt_context: Defending divine justice, Elihu insisted that wrongdoing can never be attributed to God.
question: Who said that it is far from God to do wickedness?

speaker: Elihu
quote_text: If you have sinned, what do you accomplish against him?
prompt_context: Elihu argued that human righteousness or wickedness does not alter God's being in the way Job seemed to assume.
question: Who asked Job what his sin or righteousness accomplished against God?

speaker: Elihu
quote_text: Behold, God is great
prompt_context: As Elihu moved toward his conclusion, he magnified God's greatness and the limits of human understanding.
question: Who said, "Behold, God is great," and that we do not know him fully?

speaker: God
quote_text: Who is this that darkens counsel?
prompt_context: At last the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind and challenged the limits of human understanding.
question: Who asked, "Who is this that darkens counsel?" out of the whirlwind?

speaker: God
quote_text: Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth?
prompt_context: God began his first speech by asking Job a series of questions about creation and cosmic order.
question: Who asked Job where he was when the earth's foundation was laid?

speaker: Job
quote_text: Behold, I am of small account
prompt_context: After God's first speech, Job answered briefly with humility and silence.
question: Who said, "Behold, I am of small account," and put his hand over his mouth?

speaker: God
quote_text: Will you even put me in the wrong?
prompt_context: In his second speech, God confronted Job about justifying himself at the expense of divine justice.
question: Who asked Job whether he would condemn God so that he might be justified?

speaker: Job
quote_text: I know that you can do all things
prompt_context: After God's speeches, Job confessed the greatness of God and admitted speaking beyond his understanding.
question: Who said, "I know that you can do all things," in his final response?

speaker: Job
quote_text: I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear
prompt_context: Job concluded his response by contrasting former secondhand knowledge with his present encounter before God.
question: Who said that formerly he had heard of God by the hearing of the ear but now his eye saw him?

speaker: God
quote_text: My anger burns against you
prompt_context: After Job's restoration began, the Lord addressed Job's friends and rebuked their false speaking.
question: Who said that his anger burned against Eliphaz and his two friends because they had not spoken rightly?

speaker: God
quote_text: My servant Job shall pray for you
prompt_context: In the closing rebuke, God instructed the friends to seek Job's intercession so they would be accepted.
question: Who told Eliphaz and his friends that Job would pray for them?
